constraints.assertion: Stripe webhook signature verification must happen server-side only. Never send the webhook signing secret to client-side code.
All error responses must use RFC 9457 Problem Details format (application/problem+json). Custom error formats are not permitted. See ADR-003.

Rate limit responses (HTTP 429) must include the Retry-After header. This is a contractual obligation to API consumers per the partner integration agreement signed 2025-Q3. Omitting it violates SLA terms.

Auth tokens must use RS256 signing, not HS256. This is a compliance requirement from security review 2025-Q4 (finding SEC-2025-041). HS256 was explicitly rejected because it requires sharing the signing secret with every validating service, creating an unacceptable blast radius.

args.task_description: Design JWT authentication module for Payments API: token signing algorithm, JWT claims structure, expiry, refresh strategy, and validation middleware

args.decision: Use RS256 (RSA + SHA-256 asymmetric signing) for all JWT access tokens in the Payments API auth module
args.reasoning: RS256 is mandated by active critical constraint (id: d4b1c05d) from security review finding SEC-2025-041. HS256 was explicitly rejected because it requires sharing the signing secret with every validating service, creating an unacceptable blast radius if any downstream service is compromised. With RS256, only the auth service holds the private key; all other services validate using the public key via the JWKS endpoint. This is the correct architecture for a multi-service payments platform.
alternatives_considered: HS256 — rejected: shared secret model, blast radius too large, explicitly banned by SEC-2025-041
ES256 (ECDSA P-256) — viable alternative, smaller tokens, but RS256 has broader library support and was the algorithm specified in the security review mandate
RS512 — stronger hash but no meaningful security gain over RS256 for this use case, larger tokens
args.context: Payments API, Phase: Auth Module Design. Active critical constraint d4b1c05d mandates RS256. Security review 2025-Q4 finding SEC-2025-041 is the originating requirement. Post-deployment verification checklist also confirms JWT validation must reject HS256-signed tokens.

args.decision: JWT access token structure: standard + custom claims with 15-minute expiry; opaque refresh tokens with rotation, family tracking, and 30-second grace window
args.reasoning: Short-lived access tokens (15 min) minimise the window of exposure for a stolen token. Opaque refresh tokens (not JWTs) are stored server-side so they can be revoked instantly — JWTs cannot be revoked without a blocklist. Refresh token rotation (each use issues a new token, invalidates the old) limits replay attacks. Family tracking (replay of a revoked token kills the entire family) detects token theft. The 30-second grace window handles legitimate network retries without creating a meaningful attack window. The tenant_id claim enables rate-limit tier lookups from Redis without an extra DB call per request.
alternatives_considered: Long-lived access tokens (hours/days) — rejected: stolen tokens remain valid too long for a payments context
JWT refresh tokens — rejected: cannot be server-side revoked without a blocklist, defeating the purpose
No refresh tokens (re-authenticate each time) — rejected: poor UX and higher load on the auth service
Sliding expiry on access tokens — rejected: complicates stateless validation and audit trails
args.context: Payments API auth module. Context briefing confirmed: refresh tokens are opaque, stored server-side with metadata; rotation with family tracking; 30-second grace window. Rate limiting uses tenant_id claim for tier lookup.

args.decision: Token validation middleware uses JWKS endpoint for public key resolution, rejects HS256 tokens explicitly, and returns RFC 9457 Problem Details for all auth errors distinguishing 401 vs 403
args.reasoning: JWKS-based validation decouples key rotation from service deployments — rotating the key pair only requires updating the JWKS endpoint, not redeploying every service. Explicit HS256 rejection is required by the post-deployment verification checklist and guards against algorithm confusion attacks. RFC 9457 error format is mandated by active high-severity constraint (ea913651). Distinguishing 401 (no/invalid/expired token) from 403 (valid token, insufficient scope) is required by the error-handling ADR-003 and aids client-side error handling.
alternatives_considered: Embed public key in each service config — rejected: key rotation requires redeployment of all services
Single shared validation library with hardcoded key — rejected: same rotation problem, also a single point of failure
Opaque token validation via introspection endpoint — rejected: adds latency on every request, auth service becomes hot path bottleneck
args.context: Payments API auth module. Active constraints: RS256 mandatory (critical), RFC 9457 errors mandatory (high). Post-deployment checklist requires HS256 rejection test. Error handling ADR-003 requires 401/403 distinction.

args.summary: Designed the Payments API authentication module from scratch (greenfield — no prior checkpoints). Key decisions: (1) RS256 signing mandated by critical constraint SEC-2025-041; (2) JWT access tokens with 15-min expiry, opaque rotating refresh tokens with family tracking and 30s grace window; (3) JWKS-based validation middleware with explicit HS256 rejection, RFC 9457 error responses, and 401/403 distinction. Full ADR produced and delivered to user.
next_steps: Implement the auth service token issuance endpoint (POST /auth/token)
Implement the JWKS endpoint (GET /.well-known/jwks.json)
Implement the refresh token endpoint (POST /auth/token/refresh) with rotation + family tracking
Implement the validation middleware and wire it into the Payments API route stack
Write integration tests: valid RS256 token accepted, HS256 token rejected, expired token rejected, revoked refresh token kills family
Set up key rotation automation (key-rotation service referenced in context briefing)
Resolve SEC-041 and SEC-042 before production deployment (per timeline constraint in context)
open_questions: What is the RSA key size? 2048-bit is standard minimum; 4096-bit may be preferred for payments compliance — needs confirmation from security team
What is the refresh token TTL? Context briefing did not specify — suggest 7 days for standard, 30 days for remember-me flows
Is there a token blocklist/revocation list for access tokens (e.g. on logout or key compromise)? Not mentioned in context — needs decision
Which Redis instance is used for refresh token storage — same as rate-limit state or separate? Failure modes differ
Are there machine-to-machine (M2M) clients that need client_credentials flow, or is this user-facing only?
Does the JWKS endpoint need to be publicly accessible or internal-only?

args.assertion: JWT validation middleware must explicitly reject tokens signed with HS256 or any symmetric algorithm. Only RS256 (or stronger asymmetric algorithms) are permitted. This must be verified in the post-deployment test suite.
args.evidence: Post-deployment verification checklist explicitly states: 'Confirm JWT validation rejects HS256-signed tokens'. Active constraint d4b1c05d mandates RS256. Algorithm confusion attacks (where an attacker crafts an HS256 token using the public key as the HMAC secret) are a known CVE class against JWT libraries that do not explicitly allowlist algorithms.

# Architecture Decision Record
## ADR-004 — Payments API Authentication Module

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **ID** | ADR-004 |
| **Status** | ✅ Accepted |
| **Date** | 2025 (Auth Module Design Phase) |
| **Authors** | AI Engineer (session `alpha-auth-module-design-001`) |
| **Checkpoint** | `08e9f909-884f-4c89-a4ec-f490554ae0c3` |
| **Supersedes** | — |
| **Related** | ADR-003 (Error Handling), SEC-2025-041, SEC-2025-042 |

---

## Context

The Payments API requires a robust authentication module. This is a greenfield design with no prior auth checkpoints. The following hard constraints were discovered in organisational memory before any design decisions were made:

| Constraint ID | Scope | Severity | Assertion |
|---|---|---|---|
| `d4b1c05d` | auth | **CRITICAL** | Auth tokens **must** use RS256. HS256 is explicitly banned (SEC-2025-041). |
| `ea913651` | error-handling | **HIGH** | All error responses **must** use RFC 9457 Problem Details format. |
| `61bf991d` | rate-limiting | **HIGH** | HTTP 429 responses **must** include `Retry-After` header (partner SLA obligation). |
| `0ae78fea` | integration | **CRITICAL** | Webhook signature verification must be server-side only. |

The auth service operates in a multi-service environment where downstream services (rate limiter, payments processor, webhook handler) all need to validate tokens independently. The signing algorithm choice has direct architectural implications for every service in the platform.

---

## Decision 1 — Token Signing Algorithm: RS256

### Decision
All JWT access tokens will be signed using **RS256** (RSASSA-PKCS1-v1_5 with SHA-256), an asymmetric algorithm.

### Justification

**This is non-negotiable.** Active critical constraint `d4b1c05d` mandates RS256 based on security review finding **SEC-2025-041** (Q4 2025). HS256 was explicitly evaluated and rejected.

| Property | HS256 (Rejected) | RS256 (Chosen) |
|---|---|---|
| Key model | Shared symmetric secret | Private key signs / Public key verifies |
| Secret distribution | Every validating service needs the secret | Only auth service holds the private key |
| Blast radius if compromised | **All services** can forge tokens | Only the auth service |
| Key rotation | Requires coordinated secret rotation across all services | Update JWKS endpoint only |
| Algorithm confusion attack surface | Vulnerable if library doesn't pin algorithm | Mitigated by explicit algorithm allowlist |
| Token size | ~300 bytes | ~500 bytes (acceptable trade-off) |

**ES256** (ECDSA P-256) was also considered — it produces smaller tokens and is cryptographically strong — but RS256 has broader library support and was the algorithm explicitly named in the security mandate. ES256 can be adopted in a future key rotation cycle if token size becomes a concern.

### Key Management
- **Key size**: RSA-2048 minimum; RSA-4096 recommended for payments compliance *(open question — confirm with security team)*
- **JWKS endpoint**: `GET /.well-known/jwks.json` — publicly accessible, cached by consumers with a short TTL
- **Key rotation**: Automated via the key-rotation service. New key pair is added to JWKS before the old one is retired (overlap window prevents validation failures during rotation)
- **Algorithm allowlist**: The validation middleware **must** explicitly allowlist `["RS256"]` — never use `algorithms: "auto"` or accept the `alg` header claim at face value (algorithm confusion attack vector)

---

## Decision 2 — JWT Access Token Structure

### Token Header
```json
{
  "alg": "RS256",
  "typ": "JWT",
  "kid": "key-2025-q4-001"
}
```
> `kid` (Key ID) is mandatory — it allows the validation middleware to select the correct public key from the JWKS endpoint during key rotation overlap periods.

### Token Payload (Claims)

```json
{
  // ── Registered Claims (RFC 7519) ──────────────────────────────
  "iss": "https://auth.payments.example",        // Issuer — validated by all services
  "sub": "usr_01J8X...",                          // Subject — opaque user/client ID
  "aud": ["https://api.payments.example"],        // Audience — prevents token reuse across APIs
  "iat": 1735689600,                              // Issued At (Unix timestamp)
  "exp": 1735690500,                              // Expiry = iat + 900 (15 minutes)
  "jti": "01J8X-abc123-...",                      // JWT ID — unique per token, used for revocation

  // ── Custom Claims ─────────────────────────────────────────────
  "tenant_id": "tenant_xyz",                      // Tenant — drives rate-limit tier lookup in Redis
  "scope": ["payments:write", "payments:read"],   // OAuth 2.0 scopes — drives 401 vs 403 decisions
  "user_role": "merchant",                        // Coarse role for logging/audit
  "session_id": "sess_01J8X..."                   // Links to server-side session for revocation
}
```

### Claim Design Rationale

| Claim | Why It's Here |
|---|---|
| `kid` (header) | Enables zero-downtime key rotation — middleware picks the right public key |
| `aud` | Prevents a token issued for Service A being replayed against Service B |
| `jti` | Enables token revocation (e.g. on logout) via a Redis blocklist without invalidating all tokens |
| `tenant_id` | Rate-limit middleware reads this to look up tier (Standard/Premium/Enterprise) from Redis cache — avoids a DB call per request |
| `scope` | Enables the middleware to distinguish 401 (no valid token) from 403 (valid token, wrong scope) — required by error-handling constraint |
| `session_id` | Links the access token to a server-side session record, enabling session-level revocation |

### What Is Deliberately Excluded
- **No PII** (email, name, phone) — tokens may appear in logs; PII in tokens creates a compliance liability
- **No payment instrument data** — never in a token
- **No mutable user attributes** — role changes take effect at next token issuance, not mid-token-lifetime

---

## Decision 3 — Expiry and Refresh Strategy

### Access Token Expiry: 15 Minutes
Short-lived tokens minimise the exposure window for a stolen token. In a payments context, a 15-minute window is the right balance between security and UX. Longer-lived tokens (hours/days) were rejected.

### Refresh Token Design

Refresh tokens are **opaque strings** (not JWTs), stored server-side with metadata. This is a deliberate architectural choice:

| Property | JWT Refresh Token (Rejected) | Opaque Refresh Token (Chosen) |
|---|---|---|
| Revocability | Cannot revoke without a blocklist | Instantly revocable — just delete the record |
| Storage | Stateless | Server-side (Redis + persistent DB) |
| Inspection | Self-contained | Requires a DB lookup |
| Security on theft | Valid until expiry | Revocable immediately |

### Refresh Token Lifecycle

```
[Client]                    [Auth Service]                  [Redis / DB]
   │                              │                               │
   │── POST /auth/token/refresh ──▶                               │
   │      { refresh_token: "..." }│                               │
   │                              │── lookup token record ───────▶│
   │                              │◀─ found: valid, family_id ───│
   │                              │                               │
   │                              │── mark old token USED ───────▶│
   │                              │── issue new access token      │
   │                              │── issue new refresh token ───▶│ (store with same family_id)
   │                              │── start 30s grace window      │
   │◀─ { access_token, refresh_token } ──                         │
   │                              │                               │
   │  [If old token replayed within 30s grace window]             │
   │── POST /auth/token/refresh ──▶                               │
   │      { refresh_token: "OLD" }│── still valid (grace) ───────▶│
   │◀─ { new tokens } ───────────│                               │
   │                              │                               │
   │  [If old token replayed AFTER grace window]                  │
   │── POST /auth/token/refresh ──▶                               │
   │      { refresh_token: "OLD" }│── REVOKED token detected ────▶│
   │                              │── KILL entire family ─────────▶│ (theft detected)
   │◀─ 401 Unauthorized ─────────│                               │
```

**Key properties:**
- **Rotation**: Every refresh issues a new refresh token and invalidates the old one
- **Family tracking**: All refresh tokens from the same login share a `family_id`. If a revoked token is replayed, the entire family is killed — this detects token theft
- **Grace window**: 30 seconds after rotation, the old token is still accepted — handles legitimate network retries without creating a meaningful attack window
- **Suggested TTL**: 7 days standard / 30 days for explicit "remember me" *(open question — confirm with product)*

---

## Decision 4 — Token Validation Middleware

### Middleware Pipeline (per request)

```
Incoming Request
      │
      ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ 1. EXTRACT                                              │
│    Read Authorization header                            │
│    Reject if missing or not "Bearer <token>"            │
│    → 401 { type: /errors/missing-token }                │
└────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────┘
                         │
                         ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ 2. DECODE HEADER (without verification)                 │
│    Read `kid` claim from JWT header                     │
│    Reject if `alg` ≠ "RS256"  ← EXPLICIT CHECK         │
│    → 401 { type: /errors/invalid-token }                │
└────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────┘
                         │
                         ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ 3. FETCH PUBLIC KEY                                     │
│    Look up `kid` in local JWKS cache (TTL: 5 min)       │
│    On cache miss: fetch /.well-known/jwks.json          │
│    On unknown `kid`: 401 { type: /errors/invalid-token }│
└────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────┘
                         │
                         ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ 4. VERIFY SIGNATURE                                     │
│    Verify RS256 signature with public key               │
│    Algorithm allowlist: ["RS256"] — never auto-detect   │
│    → 401 { type: /errors/invalid-token }                │
└────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────┘
                         │
                         ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ 5. VALIDATE STANDARD CLAIMS                             │
│    exp  → not expired                                   │
│    iss  → matches expected issuer                       │
│    aud  → matches this service's audience               │
│    iat  → not in the future (clock skew: ±30s)          │
│    → 401 { type: /errors/expired-token }  (if expired)  │
│    → 401 { type: /errors/invalid-token }  (otherwise)   │
└────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────┘
                         │
                         ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ 6. CHECK REVOCATION (optional, for sensitive endpoints) │
│    Look up `jti` in Redis revocation set                │
│    → 401 { type: /errors/revoked-token }                │
└────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────┘
                         │
                         ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ 7. SCOPE CHECK (route-specific)                         │
│    Verify required scope is present in token            │
│    → 403 { type: /errors/insufficient-scope }           │
│       ↑ NOTE: 403 not 401 — valid token, wrong perms    │
└────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────┘
                         │
                         ▼
                  Attach decoded token
                  to request context
                  (req.auth = { sub, tenant_id, scope, ... })
                         │
                         ▼
                  Next middleware / handler
```

### Error Response Format (RFC 9457 — Mandatory)

All auth errors **must** use `application/problem+json` per active constraint `ea913651`:

```json
// 401 — No token
{
  "type": "https://api.payments.example/errors/missing-token",
  "title": "Authentication Required",
  "status": 401,
  "detail": "No Bearer token was provided in the Authorization header.",
  "trace_id": "01J8X-abc123"
}

// 401 — Invalid/expired token
{
  "type": "https://api.payments.example/errors/expired-token",
  "title": "Token Expired",
  "status": 401,
  "detail": "The access token expired at 2025-01-01T00:15:00Z. Please refresh your token.",
  "trace_id": "01J8X-abc123"
}

// 403 — Valid token, wrong scope
{
  "type": "https://api.payments.example/errors/insufficient-scope",
  "title": "Forbidden",
  "status": 403,
  "detail": "This endpoint requires the 'payments:write' scope.",
  "required_scope": "payments:write",
  "trace_id": "01J8X-abc123"
}
```

> ⚠️ **401 vs 403 is not optional.** Returning 403 for an expired token (or 401 for a scope failure) is a bug. The distinction is required by ADR-003 and the error-handling constraint.

---

## Consequences

### Positive
- ✅ **Compliance**: Satisfies SEC-2025-041 (RS256 mandate) and SEC-2025-042 (must be resolved before production)
- ✅ **Blast radius contained**: Private key never leaves the auth service; a compromised downstream service cannot forge tokens
- ✅ **Zero-downtime key rotation**: `kid`-based JWKS lookup means rotating keys requires no service redeployment
- ✅ **Instant refresh token revocation**: Opaque server-side tokens can be killed immediately on logout or theft detection
- ✅ **Theft detection**: Refresh token family tracking detects and responds to stolen tokens automatically
- ✅ **Rate limiting integration**: `tenant_id` claim enables tier lookup without extra DB calls per request
- ✅ **Audit-friendly**: `jti`, `session_id`, and `sub` provide a complete audit trail per token

### Negative / Trade-offs
- ⚠️ **Token size**: RS256 tokens are ~500 bytes vs ~300 bytes for HS256 — acceptable, but worth monitoring in high-throughput scenarios
- ⚠️ **Auth service is critical path for issuance**: Single point of failure for token issuance — mitigated by horizontal scaling
- ⚠️ **Redis dependency

args.task_description: Implement rate limiting for the Payments API, including algorithm selection, response headers, and interaction with authentication

items.summary: [Knowledge] architecture: **: rate limiting uses a dedicated Redis cluster separate from the session store, to isolate failure domains. 4. **Fallback on Redis failure**: fail-open with WARNING-level logging. We accept temporary over-limit requests rather than blocking all traffic when Redis is down.

[Knowledge] error-handling: ype URI returns a human-readable HTML page describing the error, possible causes, and resolution steps. ### Content Type Error responses use `Content-Type: application/problem+json`. ## Consequences - All existing error handlers must be migrated to this format - Frontend clients need to handle the `application/problem+json` content type - The error type registry needs to be maintained as new error types are added - Extension fields must be prefixed with the API namespace to avoid conflicts

[Knowledge] api: sted amount.", "instance": "/v1/payments/pay_xyz789/confirm" } ``` Do NOT use custom error formats. The RFC 9457 decision was made in ADR-003 after evaluating three alternatives. ## Webhooks The API sends webhooks for payment lifecycle events: - `payment.created` - `payment.confirmed` - `payment.failed` - `payment.refunded` - `subscription.created` - `subscription.cancelled` - `subscription.payment_failed` Webhook payloads are signed with HMAC-SHA256. The signature is in the `X-Webhook-Signature` header. ## Idempotency All POST endpoints support idempotency via the `Idempotency-Key` header. Keys are stored for 24 hours. Replayed requests return the original response with a `X-Idempotent-Replayed: true` header.

[Knowledge] security: - A stolen refresh token can be used indefinitely - Token theft cannot be detected - There is no mechanism to revoke compromised sessions **Recommendation:** Implement token family tracking with automatic revocation on replay detection. ## Finding SEC-2025-043: Rate Limit Header Disclosure **Severity: Medium** **Status: Informational** Rate limit headers should be included on all responses (not just 429) to allow clients to implement proactive backoff. This is also a contractual requirement per the partner integration SLA. ## Finding SEC-2025-044: Error Response Information Leakage **Severity: Low** **Status: Informational** Error responses should not include stack traces, internal service names, or database error messages in production. Use a structured error form...

Multiple partial refunds on the same payment: supported, as long as total refunded does not exceed original amount. ## Testing ### Test Cards Stripe provides test card numbers: - `[redacted-phone]242` — succeeds - `[redacted-phone]002` — declined - `[redacted-phone]995` — insufficient funds ### Webhook Testing Use `stripe listen --forward-to localhost:3000/webhooks/stripe` for local development. ## Configuration Required environment variables: - `STRIPE_SECRET_KEY` — API secret key - `STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET` — webhook endpoint signing secret - `STRIPE_API_VERSION` — pinned API version (currently `2025-12-15.ac...

constraints.assertion: Stripe webhook signature verification must happen server-side only. Never send the webhook signing secret to client-side code.
All error responses must use RFC 9457 Problem Details format (application/problem+json). Custom error formats are not permitted. See ADR-003.

Rate limit responses (HTTP 429) must include the Retry-After header. This is a contractual obligation to API consumers per the partner integration agreement signed 2025-Q3. Omitting it violates SLA terms.

Auth tokens must use RS256 signing, not HS256. This is a compliance requirement from security review 2025-Q4 (finding SEC-2025-041). HS256 was explicitly rejected because it requires sharing the signing secret with every validating service, creating an unacceptable blast radius.

args.decision: ADR-005: Rate Limiting — Sliding Window Counter algorithm, RateLimit-* response headers on all responses, Retry-After on 429s, dual-key strategy (API key primary / user ID secondary), dedicated Redis cluster, fail-open on Redis failure
args.reasoning: 1. ALGORITHM: Sliding window counter chosen over token bucket and fixed window. Fixed window has burst-at-boundary vulnerability unacceptable for a payments API. Token bucket is accurate but requires per-request float arithmetic and is harder to inspect/debug in Redis. Sliding window counter (two-bucket approximation) gives smooth rate enforcement with O(1) Redis operations and is auditable. 2. HEADERS: SEC-2025-043 security finding and the partner SLA constraint (rate-limiting constraint ID 71f92887) both mandate Retry-After on 429. The finding also requires headers on ALL responses for proactive client backoff. We adopt the IETF draft-ietf-httpapi-ratelimit-headers standard (RateLimit-Limit, RateLimit-Remaining, RateLimit-Reset) for interoperability. 3. AUTH INTERACTION: RS256 JWT is the mandated auth mechanism (critical constraint d4b1c05d). API keys are also supported per the API spec and PCI-DSS Req 8. Primary rate limit key = API key (present on all machine-to-machine calls); secondary = JWT sub claim (user ID) for browser/mobile sessions without an API key. This prevents a single compromised API key from exhausting a user's quota and vice versa. 4. REDIS: Dedicated cluster already established in architecture (separate from session store for failure domain isolation). Fail-open policy already decided — accept temporary over-limit requests rather than blocking all traffic. 5. ERROR FORMAT: 429 responses use RFC 9457 Problem Details (constraint ea913651) with type URI /problems/rate-limit-exceeded.
args.context: Project Alpha, Payments API, Phase: Rate Limiting Implementation. Active constraints: RS256 JWT (critical), RFC 9457 errors (high), Retry-After on 429 (high, SLA obligation), webhook secret server-side only (critical). Security findings: SEC-2025-043 (rate limit headers on all responses), SEC-2025-044 (no stack traces in errors). Architecture: dedicated Redis cluster for rate limiting, fail-open on Redis failure. API supports idempotency keys, HMAC-signed webhooks, Stripe integration.
alternatives_considered: Fixed window counter — rejected: burst-at-boundary attack allows 2x the limit in a short window, unacceptable for payments
Token bucket — rejected: accurate but requires floating-point state per key, harder to inspect in Redis, no meaningful advantage over sliding window for this use case
Leaky bucket — rejected: enforces strict output rate which is inappropriate for a request/response API; better suited for queue-based systems
Per-user limits only — rejected: API keys are a first-class auth primitive per PCI-DSS Req 8; keying only on user ID would allow a single API key to exhaust all users sharing that key
Per-API-key limits only — rejected: does not protect against a single user hammering the API through multiple keys or through the JWT path
Fail-closed on Redis failure — rejected: already decided in architecture; blocking all traffic on Redis outage is worse than temporary over-limit requests for a payments API

Decisions: sliding window counter algorithm (Redis two-bucket approximation), RateLimit-Limit/Remaining/Reset headers on all responses, Retry-After on all 429s (SLA constraint 71f92887 satisfied), dual-key strategy (API key primary, JWT sub secondary), dedicated Redis cluster (pre-existing architecture), fail-open on Redis failure (pre-existing architecture decision), 429 errors in RFC 9457 format (constraint ea913651 satisfied). All active constraints respected.

---

# ADR-005: Rate Limiting for the Payments API

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **ID** | ADR-005 |
| **Status** | Accepted |
| **Date** | 2026-01-15 |
| **Deciders** | Engineering (AI-assisted), to be ratified by Tech Lead |
| **Supersedes** | — |
| **Related** | ADR-003 (RFC 9457 errors), SEC-2025-043, SEC-2025-044, Constraint 71f92887 (SLA), Constraint d4b1c05d (RS256 JWT), Constraint ea913651 (RFC 9457) |

---

## Context

The Payments API is entering the rate limiting implementation phase. Several hard requirements were discovered in memory before any design choices were made:

1. **SLA obligation (Constraint 71f92887, severity: high)** — `Retry-After` *must* appear on every HTTP 429 response. This is a contractual term in the partner integration agreement signed 2025-Q3. Omitting it violates SLA terms.
2. **Security finding SEC-2025-043 (severity: medium)** — Rate limit headers must appear on *all* responses (not just 429) so clients can implement proactive backoff.
3. **Auth constraint (Constraint d4b1c05d, severity: critical)** — Tokens use RS256 signing. HS256 was explicitly rejected because sharing the signing secret with every validating service creates an unacceptable blast radius. Rate limiting must integrate with this model.
4. **Error format constraint (Constraint ea913651, severity: high)** — All error responses, including 429s, must use RFC 9457 Problem Details (`application/problem+json`). Custom error formats are not permitted.
5. **Architecture (pre-existing)** — A dedicated Redis cluster already exists for rate limiting, isolated from the session store for failure domain separation. The fail-open policy is already decided: accept temporary over-limit requests rather than blocking all traffic when Redis is unavailable.

---

## Decision

### 1. Algorithm: Sliding Window Counter

We use the **sliding window counter** algorithm, implemented as a two-bucket approximation in Redis with a single atomic Lua script per request.

**How it works:**
- Two counters are maintained per rate limit key: one for the current window and one for the previous window.
- The effective count is: `prev_count × (1 − elapsed_fraction) + curr_count`
- This gives a smooth, continuously rolling limit without the burst-at-boundary vulnerability of fixed windows.
- All reads and increments are performed atomically via a Lua script to prevent race conditions.

**Why not the alternatives:**

| Algorithm | Reason Rejected |
|---|---|
| **Fixed window counter** | Allows up to 2× the limit in a short window at the boundary. Unacceptable for a payments API where abuse has direct financial consequences. |
| **Token bucket** | Accurate, but requires floating-point state per key and is harder to inspect/debug in Redis. No meaningful advantage over sliding window for a synchronous request/response API. |
| **Leaky bucket** | Enforces a strict *output* rate, which is appropriate for queue-based systems, not for a request/response API where clients need immediate feedback. |

---

### 2. Rate Limit Response Headers

Headers follow the **IETF `draft-ietf-httpapi-ratelimit-headers`** standard for maximum client interoperability.

#### Headers present on **every response** (2xx, 4xx, 5xx)

This satisfies SEC-2025-043 and enables proactive client backoff.

```
RateLimit-Limit: 1000
RateLimit-Remaining: 743
RateLimit-Reset: 1737032400
RateLimit-Policy: 1000;w=60
```

| Header | Description |
|---|---|
| `RateLimit-Limit` | The maximum number of requests allowed in the current window. |
| `RateLimit-Remaining` | Requests remaining in the current window. Never negative; floor at 0. |
| `RateLimit-Reset` | Unix timestamp (UTC) when the current window resets. |
| `RateLimit-Policy` | Machine-readable policy descriptor: `limit;w=window_seconds`. |

#### Additional headers on **HTTP 429 only**

```
Retry-After: 37
RateLimit-Remaining: 0
```

| Header | Description |
|---|---|
| `Retry-After` | Seconds until the client may retry. **Required by SLA (Constraint 71f92887).** Integer seconds, not HTTP-date, for unambiguous parsing. |

#### HTTP 429 body (RFC 9457 — Constraint ea913651)

```json
{
  "type": "https://api.example.com/problems/rate-limit-exceeded",
  "title": "Too Many Requests",
  "status": 429,
  "detail": "You have exceeded the rate limit of 1000 requests per 60 seconds.",
  "instance": "/v1/payments",
  "payments:rate_limit": {
    "limit": 1000,
    "window_seconds": 60,
    "retry_after_seconds": 37
  }
}
```

- `Content-Type: application/problem+json` (required by Constraint ea913651).
- Extension fields are namespaced with the `payments:` prefix per the ADR-003 convention.
- No stack traces, internal service names, or database messages (SEC-2025-044).

---

### 3. Auth Interaction: Dual-Key Rate Limiting Strategy

The API supports two authentication paths, both of which must be rate-limited independently:

| Auth path | Identity extracted | Rate limit key |
|---|---|---|
| RS256 JWT (browser/mobile sessions) | `sub` claim (user ID) | `rl:user:{sub}` |
| API key (machine-to-machine) | API key value | `rl:apikey:{hashed_key}` |

**Why dual keys, not one or the other:**

- **API key only** would fail to protect against a single user hammering the API through multiple keys, or through the JWT path.
- **User ID only** would allow a single compromised or misbehaving API key to exhaust the quota of every user associated with that key, and would not protect unauthenticated or pre-auth endpoints.
- **Dual keys** means each API key has its own independent bucket, and each authenticated user has their own independent bucket. A misbehaving API key does not affect other keys or the user's own JWT-based quota.

**Key construction:**

```
# API key path (API key is hashed with SHA-256 before use as a Redis key
# to avoid storing the raw secret in Redis)
rl:apikey:{sha256(api_key)}:{window_start_epoch}

# JWT path
rl:user:{jwt_sub}:{window_start_epoch}
```

**Unauthenticated requests** (e.g., pre-auth endpoints, webhook ingress) are keyed by IP address: `rl:ip:{client_ip}:{window_start_epoch}`. These carry a lower limit.

**Middleware ordering:**

```
Request
  └─► Auth Middleware          ← validates RS256 JWT or API key; attaches identity
        └─► Rate Limit Middleware  ← reads identity; selects key; checks Redis
              └─► Route Handler
```

Rate limiting runs *after* auth so the identity is available for key construction. This means auth failures (401) are not rate-limited — they return immediately without consuming quota, which is correct: a client with a bad token should not be penalised against its own limit.

**Limit tiers by endpoint class** (specific numbers to be confirmed with product — see open questions):

| Endpoint class | Suggested limit | Window |
|---|---|---|
| Write (POST /payments, POST /payments/:id/confirm) | 100 req | 60 s |
| Refunds (POST /payments/:id/refund) | 50 req | 60 s |
| Read (GET /payments, GET /payments/:id) | 1000 req | 60 s |
| Unauthenticated / IP-keyed | 20 req | 60 s |

---

### 4. Consistency with Prior Architectural Decisions

| Prior decision | How this ADR respects it |
|---|---|
| **RS256 JWT (critical constraint)** | Rate limit key extraction reads the `sub` claim from the already-validated RS256 JWT. No HS256 path is introduced. |
| **RFC 9457 errors (high constraint)** | 429 body uses `application/problem+json` with `type`, `title`, `status`, `detail`, `instance`, and namespaced extension fields. |
| **Retry-After on 429 (high constraint / SLA)** | `Retry-After` is emitted on every 429 response. It is also tested in the integration test suite as a required assertion. |
| **Dedicated Redis cluster** | Rate limit counters use the pre-existing dedicated Redis cluster, not the session store. |
| **Fail-open on Redis failure** | If the Redis call times out or errors, the request is allowed through with a `WARNING`-level log entry. `RateLimit-Remaining` is omitted from the response in this case to avoid misleading clients. |
| **Idempotency keys** | Replayed idempotent requests (returning `X-Idempotent-Replayed: true`) still consume quota. The original request already consumed a slot; the replay is a new network call and should be counted. |
| **Webhook HMAC verification (critical constraint)** | Webhook ingress endpoints are rate-limited by IP only and do not participate in the API key / user ID dual-key scheme. The webhook signing secret remains server-side only. |

---

## Consequences

### Positive
- Smooth rate enforcement with no burst-at-boundary vulnerability.
- SLA contractual obligation (`Retry-After`) is met on every 429.
- Clients can implement proactive backoff using headers on all responses (SEC-2025-043 resolved).
- API key and user quotas are fully isolated — a misbehaving integration cannot starve other consumers.
- Consistent with all four active constraints and both relevant security findings.
- Atomic Redis Lua script prevents race conditions under concurrent load.

### Negative / Trade-offs
- Sliding window approximation (two-bucket) can under-count by up to ~10% at window boundaries compared to a true sliding log. This is acceptable for a payments API; a true sliding log would require O(n) Redis memory per key.
- Running rate limiting after auth means the auth middleware is not itself rate-limited. A DDoS of malformed tokens could still reach the auth layer. Mitigation: IP-based rate limiting at the load balancer/WAF layer (out of scope for this ADR).
- Dual-key strategy doubles Redis writes per request. At expected traffic volumes this is negligible; revisit if Redis CPU becomes a bottleneck.

---

## Open Questions

The following must be resolved before implementation is complete (also saved to checkpoint `97e835d6`):

1. **Numeric limit tiers** — What are the exact request-per-window limits for each endpoint class? (Product/commercial decision.)
2. **Webhook exemption** — Should webhook delivery endpoints have a separate higher limit or be fully exempt?
3. **Burst allowance** — Is there a short-burst multiplier on top of the sustained sliding window rate?
4. **Cross-version state** — Should rate limit counters be shared across API versions (v1, v2) or isolated per version?
5. **Partner SLA header names** — Does the 2025-Q3 partner agreement specify exact header names, or only the presence of `Retry-After`?

---

## Implementation Checklist

- [ ] Write Redis Lua script for atomic two-bucket sliding window increment + read
- [ ] Implement `RateLimitMiddleware` — extracts identity, selects Redis key, calls Lua script
- [ ] Add `RateLimit-*` headers to global response interceptor (all status codes)
- [ ] Add `Retry-After` header injection on 429 path only
- [ ] Wire middleware after `AuthMiddleware`, before route handlers
- [ ] Implement fail-open path with `WARNING` log and header omission
- [ ] Integration tests: normal headers, 429 + `Retry-After`, Redis failure fail-open, API key isolation, user ID isolation
- [ ] Document rate limit tiers in the API reference

constraints.assertion: Stripe webhook signature verification must happen server-side only. Never send the webhook signing secret to client-side code.
All error responses must use RFC 9457 Problem Details format (application/problem+json). Custom error formats are not permitted. See ADR-003.

args.task_description: Design global error handling strategy for Payments API consistent with Phase 1 auth and Phase 2 rate limiting ADRs

args.action: Design and implement a global error handling middleware for the Payments API that: uses RFC 9457 Problem Details format (application/problem+json) for all error responses; returns 401 for missing/invalid/expired tokens and 403 for valid tokens with insufficient permissions; returns 429 with Retry-After, X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining, and X-RateLimit-Reset headers for rate limit errors; never exposes internal stack traces or signing secrets in error bodies; logs all errors with request IDs for audit trail compliance
args.context: Phase 3 of Payments API build. Phase 1 established RS256 JWT auth with @payments/auth-middleware. Phase 2 established sliding-window rate limiting with Redis. Active constraints require RFC 9457 format (high), Retry-After header on 429 (high), RS256 signing (critical), and webhook secret server-side only (critical).

toolCalls.error: HTTP 422: tool_name and action_description are required

args.action: Return 401 for unauthenticated requests (missing, malformed, or expired JWT) and 403 for authenticated requests that lack required permissions or roles

args.decision: ADR-003: Global Error Handling Strategy for Payments API — RFC 9457 Problem Details format with layered middleware architecture
args.reasoning: All four active constraints drive this design: (1) CRITICAL: RS256 JWT signing must be preserved — error responses must never leak token internals or signing material. (2) HIGH: All error responses must use RFC 9457 Problem Details (application/problem+json) per ADR-003 constraint. (3) HIGH: Rate limit 429 responses must include Retry-After header per partner SLA contractual obligation. (4) CRITICAL: Webhook signing secrets must never appear in error bodies. Additionally, PCI-DSS Requirement 10 mandates all requests are logged with timestamps, user IDs, and request IDs. The design uses a single global error handler middleware that normalises all thrown errors into Problem Details objects, with specialised sub-handlers for auth errors (401 vs 403 distinction), rate limit errors (mandatory headers), validation errors (400), and payment domain errors (402, 409, 422).
alternatives_considered: Custom JSON error format — rejected: violates active HIGH constraint requiring RFC 9457
Per-route error handling — rejected: inconsistent, harder to audit, misses cross-cutting concerns like logging and header injection
HTTP status codes only with no body — rejected: insufficient for partner integrations and debugging
Exposing internal error messages directly — rejected: PCI-DSS and security constraints prohibit leaking internals
args.context: Phase 3 of Payments API. Phase 1 (auth): RS256 JWT via @payments/auth-middleware, 15-min access tokens, deny-list for revoked tokens, API keys with pk_live_/pk_test_ prefixes. Phase 2 (rate limiting): sliding window, Redis cluster, tiered limits (Standard 100/min, Premium 500/min, Enterprise custom), Retry-After mandatory per SLA. Active constraints: RS256 (critical), webhook secret server-side only (critical), RFC 9457 error format (high), Retry-After on 429 (high).

args.summary: Phase 3 error handling design completed. All 4 active constraints reviewed: (1) RS256 JWT signing - critical, (2) RFC 9457 Problem Details format - high, (3) Retry-After on 429 - high, (4) Webhook secret server-side only - critical. Designed global error handling middleware with layered architecture: AuthErrorHandler (401/403 distinction), RateLimitErrorHandler (mandatory headers), PaymentDomainErrorHandler, ValidationErrorHandler, and catch-all GlobalErrorHandler. All responses use application/problem+json. No internal details or secrets exposed in error bodies. Full audit logging on every error for PCI-DSS Req 10 compliance.
next_steps: Implement GlobalErrorMiddleware class in src/middleware/error-handler.ts
Implement AuthError subclasses (UnauthenticatedError, ForbiddenError) in src/errors/auth.ts
Implement RateLimitError with header injection in src/errors/rate-limit.ts
Implement PaymentDomainError hierarchy in src/errors/payment.ts
Wire middleware into Express app after all route handlers
Write integration tests covering all error scenarios
Verify PCI-DSS audit log fields on every error response path
open_questions: Should 503 Redis-unavailable errors for rate limiting fail-open (current decision) or fail-closed? Confirm with team before production.
Should webhook signature errors return 400 or 401? Lean 400 (bad request, not auth failure) but confirm.
Do Enterprise tier rate limit errors need custom Retry-After logic or same as Standard/Premium?
